content.text: A tool definition is the primary API contract between an LLM and a tool executor. When an agent framework sends tool definitions to a language model, the model learns from those schemas what arguments to produce. If a tool's JSON Schema changes without a corresponding update to the runtime validator or the agent prompt, the LLM will generate arguments that the executor rejects — silently aborting tool calls mid-workflow. Schema contracts formalize this boundary, version it, and enforce compatibility rules in CI.
What it is

A schema contract for a tool definition is a versioned JSON Schema document that specifies the exact shape of the arguments a tool accepts and the shape of the response it returns. The contract is published to a schema registry — either a dedicated service like Confluent Schema Registry or a simpler file-based registry in the repository — and referenced both when the tool definition is sent to the LLM and when the tool executor validates incoming arguments at runtime.

The contract enforces three guarantees. First, the tool definition sent to the LLM and the validator in the tool executor always reference the same schema version — preventing schema drift between the two sides of the function call. Second, schema changes follow a backward-compatibility discipline: adding optional fields is allowed; removing required fields or changing types requires a version bump and a migration plan. Third, the CI pipeline prevents merging schema changes that break the registered compatibility rules.

Tool definitions for LLMs typically follow the OpenAI function-calling format: a JSON object with a name, description, and parameters sub-object conforming to JSON Schema Draft 7. Schema contracts extend this by adding versioning metadata, registering the schema in a known location, and automating compatibility checks.
Why it matters

LLMs are sensitive to tool schema changes in ways that are difficult to predict. When a required field is renamed, the model continues generating the old field name because its behavior is shaped by the schema it received during the last conversation — even if the underlying implementation now expects a different name. The mismatch produces a validation error in the tool executor, the tool call fails silently or with a cryptic error, and the agent either hallucinates a result or enters an error recovery loop.

Schema drift between the definition shown to the LLM and the validator enforced by the executor is the most common category of tool-related production incidents in agentic systems. Because tool definitions and tool executors are often maintained in different parts of the codebase — sometimes by different teams — they tend to diverge incrementally with every refactor. A schema contract makes the shared specification the single source of truth, eliminating the drift.

Backward compatibility rules matter because multiple deployed versions of a consumer (an agent using the tool) may be in flight simultaneously. A schema change that breaks older agent versions will cause failures in any active agent session that was initialized before the change was deployed. Compatibility checks in CI prevent these breaks from reaching production.
Benefits
Single source of truth: the registered schema is the authoritative definition used both for LLM tool-call generation and for runtime validation.
Breaking change prevention: incompatible schema changes are blocked at CI before they can affect any deployed consumer.
Version history: the registry provides a complete audit trail of every schema change, when it was made, and whether it was backward compatible.
Automated documentation: the registered schema is machine-readable API documentation for the tool's interface.
Safe refactoring: developers can confidently rename internal code while keeping the external schema stable, decoupling internal from external representation.
Reduced debugging time: runtime validation errors include the schema version, making it straightforward to identify whether a mismatch caused the failure.
Trade-offs
Introducing a schema registry adds infrastructure overhead — a new service to deploy, configure, and maintain.
Schema compatibility rules can feel bureaucratic when rapid iteration is needed; teams sometimes bypass them, defeating their purpose.
JSON Schema Draft 7 does not have native support for recursive or highly polymorphic types that some tools require — workarounds can be verbose.

The LLM's interpretation of a schema is not identical to a JSON Schema validator's interpretation; a schema that is technically valid may still produce unexpected model behavior if the description fields are ambiguous.
Schema versioning adds cognitive overhead to tool development: developers must understand backward-compatibility rules and know when a version bump is required.
How it works
Schema authoring

Each tool's schema is authored as a standalone JSON Schema file under a tools/schemas/ directory. The schema includes all JSON Schema validation rules (types, formats, patterns, required fields) plus tool-specific metadata: the tool name, a human-readable description used by the LLM to understand the tool's purpose, and a version field.
Schema registration

When a tool schema is merged to the main branch, a CI step registers it in the schema registry. The registry records the schema under the tool name and version key and evaluates the compatibility mode (BACKWARD, FORWARD, or FULL) by diffing the new schema against the previously registered version. If compatibility is violated, registration fails and the merge is blocked.
Runtime validation

When the tool executor receives an argument payload from the LLM, it fetches the current schema version from the registry (or from a local cache populated at startup) and validates the payload using a JSON Schema validator. Validation failures are returned as structured error responses, not raised as unhandled exceptions, so the agent can decide how to handle them — typically by requesting the LLM to retry with corrected arguments.
Compatibility modes

BACKWARD compatibility means new schema consumers can read data produced by the old schema — this allows adding optional fields and removing optional fields. FORWARD compatibility means old schema consumers can read data produced by the new schema. FULL compatibility (both directions) is the most restrictive and is appropriate for stable, shared tool APIs. For rapidly evolving tools in a single-team context, no compatibility check (free-form versioning) may be appropriate with a hard requirement for a major version bump on breaking changes.
Architecture

attrs.alt: Schema contract lifecycle: tool definition registered to schema registry, CI gate checks compatibility, LLM tool calls validated at runtime

content.text: The diagram shows the three phases of schema contract lifecycle. The tool definition is registered to the schema registry during CI, which checks backward compatibility against the previously registered version. At runtime, LLM-generated tool call arguments are validated against the registered schema version. The CI gate blocks merges that introduce incompatible changes.
When to use it
Use schema contracts when a tool is called by more than one agent or version of an agent.
Use them when tool definitions are maintained by a different team than the tool executor.
Use them when tools are stable enough to warrant the versioning overhead — rapidly evolving experimental tools may not benefit.
Use FULL compatibility when the tool is a public API used by external partners or in a multi-tenant platform.
Skip formal schema contracts for private, single-use tools in early-stage prototypes where interface stability is not yet a goal.
How to implement

# tools/validate_schema_contract.py
# CI script: validates that a schema change is backward compatible

import json
import sys
from pathlib import Path
import jsonschema
from schema_registry import SchemaRegistryClient

REGISTRY_URL = "http://schema-registry.internal:8081"
TOOLS_DIR = Path("tools/schemas")

def check_compatibility(tool_name: str, new_schema: dict) -> tuple[bool, str]:
    """Returns (is_compatible, reason)."""
    client = SchemaRegistryClient(REGISTRY_URL)
    
    try:
        latest = client.get_latest_version(f"tool-{tool_name}")
    except client.SubjectNotFoundException:
        return True, "No previous version registered; first registration."
    
    old_schema = json.loads(latest.schema.schema)
    
    # Check: no required field removed
    old_required = set(old_schema.get("required", []))
    new_required = set(new_schema.get("required", []))
    removed_required = old_required - new_required
    if removed_required:
        return False, f"BREAKING: required fields removed: {removed_required}"
    
    # Check: no property type changed
    old_props = old_schema.get("properties", {})
    new_props = new_schema.get("properties", {})
    for field, old_def in old_props.items():
        if field in new_props:
            if old_def.get("type") != new_props[field].get("type"):
                return False, f"BREAKING: type of '{field}' changed from {old_def.get('type')} to {new_props[field].get('type')}"
    
    # Check: additionalProperties not loosened (false -> true is a breaking change for validators)
    if old_schema.get("additionalProperties") is False and new_schema.get("additionalProperties") is not False:
        return False, "BREAKING: additionalProperties changed from false to non-false"
    
    return True, "Backward compatible."

def main():
    changed_schemas = sys.argv[1:]  # List of changed schema file paths from CI
    
    failed = False
    for schema_path in changed_schemas:
        schema = json.loads(Path(schema_path).read_text())
        tool_name = schema.get("title") or Path(schema_path).stem
        
        compatible, reason = check_compatibility(tool_name, schema)
        if compatible:
            print(f"[PASS] {tool_name}: {reason}")
        else:
            print(f"[FAIL] {tool_name}: {reason}", file=sys.stderr)
            failed = True
    
    sys.exit(1 if failed else 0)

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
Examples

# tests/unit/test_tool_schema_contracts.py
# Unit tests for tool schema correctness (not registry integration)

import json
import pytest
import jsonschema
from pathlib import Path

SCHEMA_DIR = Path("tools/schemas")

@pytest.fixture(params=list(SCHEMA_DIR.glob("*.json")))
def tool_schema(request):
    return json.loads(request.param.read_text())

def test_schema_is_valid_json_schema(tool_schema):
    """Every tool schema must be a valid JSON Schema Draft 7 document."""
    jsonschema.Draft7Validator.check_schema(tool_schema)

def test_schema_has_title(tool_schema):
    """The title is used as the LLM function name — it must be present and snake_case."""
    assert "title" in tool_schema
    name = tool_schema["title"]
    assert "_" in name or name.isalpha(), f"Tool name '{name}' should be snake_case"
    assert " " not in name

def test_schema_has_description(tool_schema):
    """A non-empty description improves LLM tool-selection accuracy."""
    assert tool_schema.get("description"), "Tool schema must have a non-empty description"
    assert len(tool_schema["description"]) >= 20, "Description is too short to be useful to the LLM"

def test_schema_disallows_additional_properties(tool_schema):
    """additionalProperties: false prevents LLM hallucinated fields from passing validation."""
    params = tool_schema.get("properties", {})
    if params:  # Skip check for zero-parameter tools
        assert tool_schema.get("additionalProperties") is False, (
            "additionalProperties must be false to prevent hallucinated fields"
        )

def test_required_fields_are_documented(tool_schema):
    """Every required field must have a description to help the LLM populate it correctly."""
    required = tool_schema.get("required", [])
    properties = tool_schema.get("properties", {})
    for field in required:
        assert field in properties, f"Required field '{field}' not in properties"
        assert properties[field].get("description"), (
            f"Required field '{field}' has no description"
        )
Real-world example

A document intelligence platform maintains 12 tool definitions across three agent types: a reading agent that extracts tables and text, a writing agent that creates and edits documents, and a routing agent that dispatches tasks between them. Each tool definition is versioned in a tools/schemas/ directory and registered to a self-hosted Confluent Schema Registry.

When the write_document tool needed an additional required field (document_type) to support a new file format, the developer added the field to the schema and pushed to a feature branch. The CI schema compatibility check detected that adding a required field breaks backward compatibility — existing consumers do not send this field. The check failed, and the developer was directed to make document_type optional with a default value instead. This approach maintained backward compatibility while enabling the new feature without a coordinated upgrade of all consumers.
Common mistakes
Not setting additionalProperties: false — allows LLM-hallucinated fields to pass validation and reach the executor with unexpected arguments.
Weak or missing description fields — the LLM learns how to use the tool from the description; a vague or absent description leads to mis-populated arguments.
Storing tool schemas inline in code rather than as versioned files — makes it impossible to track what changed and when.
Skipping the compatibility check in CI — defeats the purpose of schema contracts entirely.
Using overly strict patterns (strict regex on string fields) — creates fragile schemas that the LLM cannot reliably satisfy.
Not validating LLM-produced arguments at runtime — schema contracts without runtime validation only protect against developer changes, not model drift.
Best practices
Write tool descriptions as instructions to the LLM: explain what the tool does, when to use it, and what each parameter means in plain language.
Use additionalProperties: false on the parameters object to prevent hallucinated fields from reaching the executor.
Make new parameters optional with defaults rather than required, preserving backward compatibility.
Validate every LLM tool call argument at the executor entry point before executing any side effects.
Run schema validation as the first step in the tool executor, returning a structured error that the agent can use to retry with corrected arguments.
Version schema files using semantic versioning in the filename (search_v2.json) or in the $id field.
Automate schema publication to the registry from CI on merges to main, not manually.
Performance & scalability

Schema validation at the tool executor entry point adds microseconds to tool execution latency — negligible compared to tool execution time. For high-throughput tool services processing hundreds of calls per second, schema validation should use a compiled validator (compiled JSON Schema Draft 7 validators are significantly faster than interpreted ones) and should validate against a schema loaded at startup, not fetched from the registry on every call.

The schema registry itself is a low-traffic service: it is only called on registration (CI events) and at service startup (to load the current schema). It does not need to be on the hot path of tool execution. A simple key-value store (Redis, DynamoDB, or even a Git-backed file store) is sufficient for most teams.
Tooling & ecosystem
jsonschema (Python)
: reference JSON Schema validator for Python; used for runtime tool argument validation.
ajv (JavaScript)
: the fastest JSON Schema validator for Node.js; supports compilation for hot-path validation.
Confluent Schema Registry
: battle-tested schema registry originally designed for Avro schemas; supports JSON Schema with compatibility checking.
AWS Glue Schema Registry
: managed schema registry on AWS; supports JSON Schema and integrates with EventBridge and Kinesis.
buf (Protobuf)
: if tools use Protobuf for serialization instead of JSON, buf provides schema registry and breaking-change detection.
openapi-diff
: detects breaking changes between two OpenAPI specs; can be repurposed for JSON Schema diffing in CI.

Security & reliability

Schema contracts are a defensive reliability mechanism: they prevent malformed tool arguments — whether from LLM hallucination, prompt injection, or model drift — from reaching the executor's business logic. This is especially important for tools that execute code, write files, or call external APIs, where a malformed argument could trigger unintended side effects.

Runtime schema validation should run before any authorization or permission check fails and before any side-effecting operation begins. Validation errors must be returned as structured responses to the agent rather than propagated as unhandled exceptions, which could expose internal stack traces or leave the agent in an inconsistent state. Schema validation logs should be included in the security audit trail for tool-calling events.
Key takeaways
A schema contract formalizes the interface between the LLM tool definition and the tool executor, making both sides reference the same versioned specification.
additionalProperties: false and runtime validation together prevent LLM-hallucinated fields from reaching executor business logic.
Breaking changes (removing required fields, changing types) are blocked in CI by the compatibility checker.
New parameters should be optional with defaults to preserve backward compatibility across deployed consumer versions.
Tool description quality directly affects LLM accuracy; treat descriptions as interface documentation written for the model.
Schema registration at CI time, combined with startup-time cache loading, keeps the registry off the hot execution path.
Structured validation error responses (not exceptions) allow the agent to retry with corrected arguments rather than failing the entire workflow.
